coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic efficiency towards water pollutant degradation has been limited due to the intermittence of the sunlight, low light utilization and rapid recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Here a photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst of Bi2Fe4O9/Carbon-dots/g-C3N4 heterostructure has been fabricated by loading Bi2Fe4O9 on the Carbon-dots modified g-C3N4 with opened g-C3N4 layer distance, which combined large surface area and photocatalytic activity of Carbon-dots/g-C3N4, and excellent piezoelectric effect of Bi2Fe4O9 for better electron-hole separation efficiency, achieved highly efficient antibiotics and dye degradation even under very weak visible-light treatment and mechanical stress. This heterostructure may fully use wave energy and light irradiation in numerous natural water environmental conditions and enhance the catalytic degradation efficiency, even in cloudy weather or at night when the photocatalytic activity is invalidated.

head.ce:title: Weak visible-light photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst based on the Bi2Fe4O9/Carbon-dots/g-C3N4 heterostructure

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic efficiency towards water pollutant degradation has been limited due to the intermittence of the sunlight, low light utilization and rapid recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs. Here a photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst of Bi2Fe4O9/Carbon-dots/g-C3N4 heterostructure has been fabricated by loading Bi2Fe4O9 on the Carbon-dots modified g-C3N4 with opened g-C3N4 layer distance, which combined large surface area and photocatalytic activity of Carbon-dots/g-C3N4, and excellent piezoelectric effect of Bi2Fe4O9 for better electron-hole separation efficiency, achieved highly efficient antibiotics and dye degradation even under very weak visible-light treatment and mechanical stress. This heterostructure may fully use wave energy and light irradiation in numerous natural water environmental conditions and enhance the catalytic degradation efficiency, even in cloudy weather or at night when the photocatalytic activity is invalidated.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar photocatalysis has been widely investigated as a promising and efficient solution for environmental pollution elimination owing to its excellent oxidation and purification capabilities by harvesting and converting natural energy to chemical energy [1–5]. However, the restrictions of solar photocatalytic efficiency are two-fold: a) the relatively limited light utilization: solar energy is intermittent, meanwhile the absorption threshold of many semiconductor materials is usually in the ultraviolet (UV) region (300–400 nm), which only accounts for 6% of the solar spectrum [6,7], hence, the energy loss in the visible region (44%, 400–700 nm) greatly limits the utilization of solar energy; b) the rapid recombination of the photo-generated electron-hole leads to a decrease in photocatalytic activity [8,9]. Therefore, it is urgent to develop new techniques to overcome the above shortcomings.

Various attempts have been investigated for improving photocatalytic efficiency, including defect or band gap engineering [10–13], morphology maneuvering [14–16] and heterojunction constructing [10,17–20], etc. Improvements have been achieved, however, the photocatalyst still completely relies on intermittent solar energy and the recombination of the electrons and holes needs to be further inhibited. Piezoelectric materials can generate an electric charge in response to applied mechanical stress, which promotes the separation of electrons and holes and enables the use of sustainable and continuous energy, such as water wave energy, wind energy, etc. [21–23]. Piezoelectric catalytic technology can achieve efficient degradation by using the abundant low-frequency mechanical energy source in nature, thus attracting wide attention [24–28]. Therefore, photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis shows higher catalytic efficiency and has become a new research field in the latest years.

Bismuth ferrite (BiFeO3) is a typical ferroelectric material with a rhomboid twisted perovskite structure, which exhibits effective piezoelectric properties [16,29]. Meanwhile, its narrow band gap (2.1–2.8 eV) makes it suitable for photocatalytic systems [30]. Its good biocompatibility and biosafety make it an environmentally friendly material. Herein, BiFeO3 has been developed for a novel photo-piezoelectric synergetic catalyst [31,32]. However, the catalytic efficiency of pristine BiFeO3 nanoparticles are limited by their relatively small surface areas [33], their limited light response range and electron mobility [34]. Thus, in this work, efforts have been made to develop a BiFeO3-based photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalyst with visible-light response and large surface area to tackle the above shortcomings.

To improve the light utilization, Bi2Fe4O9 (BFO) instead of BiFeO3 has been synthesized, which shows higher photocatalytic activity owing to its broader light absorption, especially for light with energy higher than 2.65 eV [35–38]. To increase the surface area, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a new type of two-dimensional lamellar nanomaterial, has been employed as a supporting material for BFO [39–41]. G-C3N4 is a low-cost and environmentally friendly material with unique optical properties and low cytotoxicity [42–47]. However, the layered structure of g-C3N4 prevents the BFO adhesion in the interlayers, which limited its surface area [46–51]. In our previous work, a simple but effective hydrothermal process has been developed to open the layered distance of g-C3N4 by fabricating Carbon quantum dots (C-dots)/g-C3N4, which increased the surface area by 3.56 times [52]. Meanwhile, the g-C3N4 possesses appropriate energy-band potentials matched with BFO, hence, BiFeO3/g-C3N4 heterostructures have been proven a higher photocatalytic activity [35,36]. However, its piezoelectric and photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalytic performance remains unclear. Theoretically, a local electric field could be formed at the interface of g-C3N4 and BiFeO3 nanoparticles by the BiFeO3 piezoelectric polarization, which may enhance the carrier transport at the interface and inhibit the recombination of photo-generated carriers. Besides, as a better electron acceptor and transport carrier, C-dots can bridge the BiFeO3 and g-C3N4 to further improve the light absorption efficiency, accelerate electron transfer and electron-hole separation, and act as electron storage centres [53,54].

Herein, a photo-piezoelectric synergistic BFO/C-dots/g-C3N4 (BFO/CC) heterostructure has been fabricated via a hydrothermal method, aiming for broader light response range, better photo-generated electron-hole separation, larger surface area, and higher charge transfer efficiency. The photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalytic activities of the novel heterostructure have been symmetrically optimized. The fabricated BFO/CC shows high catalytic activity towards the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) and antibiotics under both weak UV irradiation, visible light and stirring, and shows excellent photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalytic activities, which are 8.97, 15.43, and 1.41 times higher than that under weak UV, visible light, and stirring. The BFO/CC also exhibits good photocatalytic reusability with only a 1.2% decrease in the degradation after 5 cycles. This work provides a new design of zero-dimensional/two-dimensional nanomaterial heterojunction fabrication, and may also shed some light on the development of novel photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysts.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H10O8), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), melamine (C3H6N6), Rhodamine B (RhB), tetracycline (TC), potassium hydroxide (KOH), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), and ferric nitrate nine hydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) were all purchased from Sinopril Chemical Reagents Co., LTD. 2-methoxyethanol (C3H8O2) was purchased from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., LTD.

The g-C3N4 powders were synthesized by directly heating melamine in a muffle furnace. An appropriate amount of C3H6N6 was calcined at 550 °C for 5 h to obtain massive g-C3N4 (heating rate: 10 °C/min). Subsequently, the as-prepared g-C3N4 products were collected and ground into powders.

C-dots were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. First, 20 g C6H10O8 was dissolved in 7 ml deionized water and 6 ml C2H8N2 was slowly added in drops until the solution thickens. The solution was then reacted at 180 °C for 20 h. After naturally cooling down, a small amount of deionized water was added to the anhydrous ethanol with stirring. The mixture was stored for 1–2 days, washed and centrifuged with anhydrous ethanol, and vacuum dried at 80 °C to obtain C-dots. A hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize C-dots/g-C3N4 in this work, and the details of the synthesis can be found in ESI.

For the synthesis of BFO, Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (0.005 mol) and Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (0.005 mol) were dissolved in 100 ml distilled water and 20 ml C3H8O2. Under stirring, 10 mL KOH (8 M) mineralizer was slowly added to form a dark brown suspension, and the suspension was ultrasonic treated for 30 min to obtain a highly uniform suspension and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the BFO was filtered and washed with ethanol and deionized water to remove soluble salts and then dried at 70 °C in a vacuum oven.

The hydrothermal method was used to prepare BFO/CC. BFO, C-dots, and g-C3N4 were mixed and thoroughly ground, and then the mixture was added into an autoclave with 50 mL of deionized water. The mixture was stirred for half an hour before hydrothermal heating at 180 °C for 20 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature, centrifuged, and washed. The composite was vacuum dried at 85 °C for 6 h. On this basis, BFO/CC with different mass ratios (1:3:10, 2:3:10, 5:3:10, 8:3:10, 24:1:5, 38:1:3.3) was prepared. The synthesis process and catalysis test of BFO/CC ternary heterojunction are illustrated in Fig. 1 .

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis was measured by a Bruker AXS-D2 diffractometer (Karlsruhe, Germany) equipped with monochromatic high-intensity Cu-Ka radiation (λ=0.154,056 nm). Fourier transmission infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrometer. The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were performed using an Electron 7610f Japan and Japanese electronic JEM-2100 Plus, respectively. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas were detected based on nitrogen adsorption isotherms, using an Autosorb-iQ (Quantachrome, USA). Surface chemical states of the elements and functional groups were identified with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific Nexsa).

Ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis spectra) was performed on a Shimadzu SolidSpec-3700 instrument. The electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co. Ltd, Shanghai, China) with a three-electrode cell was used to measure the photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) Nyquist plots. Optical properties were determined by photoluminescence spectra (PL, Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer) and surface photovoltage (SPV, CEL-SPS1000 surface photovoltage spectrometer).

For the degradation experiment of RhB, the 10 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 100 mL RhB solution (7.5×10−6 M) with stirring (100 rpm, 5 min). As for the degradation experiment of TC, the 25 mg photocatalyst was dispersed in 50 mL TC solution (2.25×10−5 M) with stirring (100 rpm, 5 min). And the solution was stood in darkness for 30 min before the experiment to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Weak UV light (10.2 mW/cm2, 365 nm), and visible light (12.2 mW/cm2, 420 nm) have been employed as light sources in photocatalysis. The distance between light and the reaction solution was fixed at 10 cm. For the piezoelectric measurements, a stirring apparatus (625 rpm) has been applied in the dark to avoid photocatalytic degradation. Details on piezoelectric stress have been compared in the ESI. For the photo-piezoelectric degradation experiments, the above light source was combined with stirring (625 rpm) for synergistic degradation. It is worth noting that TC degradation was measured based on absorption at 350 nm and RhB degradation based on absorption at 554 nm by WFJ-2000 spectrophotometer.

The BFO, C-dots, g-C3N4 and their composites have been characterized by XRD (Fig. 2 a). For the C-dots, a wide peak at ∼ 23° is observed, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane [55]. For g-C3N4, there are two distinct peaks at 13.1° and 27.7°, which are the (100) and (002) crystal planes of graphite phase carbon nitride (JCPDS Card 87–1526) [56]. The XRD pattern of CC is similar to that of g-C3N4, which is because of the small content of C-dots in CC composites. In the XRD pattern of BFO, most diffraction peaks belong to the orthogonal Bi2Fe4O9 structure (JCPDS Card 72–1832), and two very small diffraction peaks (31.75° and 32.07°) belong to BiFeO3 [37]. This may be because the BiFeO3 is not completely separated during washing, but its content is very low. In the XRD pattern of BFO/CC heterojunction, all the characteristic diffraction peaks belong to C-dots, g-C3N4, and BFO and no impurity phase has been detected. The above results indicate the combination of C-dots, g-C3N4, and BFO did not change the crystal structure, and the ternary heterostructure was successfully synthesized.

FT-IR was used to analyze the samples to further determine the chemical functional groups and composition of BFO/CC. As shown in Fig. 2b, the absorption peak at ∼ 808 cm−1 of g-C3N4 is attributed to the bending vibration of the CN heterocyclic ring. A typical g-C3N4 absorption between 1200 and 1700 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of the CN and C = N heterocycle, and the absorption peak at 3000 cm−1 corresponds to NH stretching vibration [56,57]. FT-IR spectra of CC are similar to the absorption peak of g-C3N4, and the absorption intensities of CN, CNC are significantly enhanced, indicating the interaction between C-dots and g-C3N4 [52]. FT-IR spectra show that the intensity of many diffraction peaks decreases after the composite, which may be caused by the low content of C-dots and g-C3N4 in BFO/CC composites.

SEM and TEM images were captured to characterize the morphology and structure of BFO and BFO/CC. Fig. 3 a provided the SEM image of BFO/CC, from which the layered g-C3N4 modified by BFO nanoparticles and C-dots can be observed. The EDX (Fig. 3b) shows Bi, Fe, O, C, and N elements distribution and weight percentage within the red box in Fig. 3a. Fig. 3c, d are the TEM and HRTEM images of BFO. The BFO is well dispersed and in its HRTEM image, the 0.252 nm lattice fringe corresponds to the (310) crystal face of BFO nanoparticles (Fig. 3d). The TEM images of g-C3N4 and C-dots are provided in ESI. In Fig. 3e, f, the TEM images of BFO/CC have been given. The g-C3N4 is a two-dimensional lamellar structure with few accumulated layers, and the cubic BFO nanoparticles and punctured C-dots are uniformly attached to the surface of g-C3N4. It can be seen from Fig. 3f that there are well-developed and clear lattice fringes, and the lattice fringes of 0.316 nm and 0.289 nm correspond to the (121) and (220) crystal planes of BFO nanoparticles, respectively. A large area of lattice fringes corresponds to the 0.224 nm lattice spacing, which matches the (003) crystal plane of g-C3N4. C-dots are circular spots with a diameter of 3–5 nm. The formation of BFO/CC composites is critical for the charge transfer between the semiconductors to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 4 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of BFO and BFO/CC and their pore size distribution. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, isotherms are type IV and the presence of hysterescent loops at higher relative pressures indicates the presence of mesoporous structures [58,59]. The specific surface area of BFO/CC (11.543 m2/g, Table S1) is significantly larger than that of BFO (7.225 m2/g), and the total pore volume of BFO/CC (0.190 cm3/g) is about 1.4 times that of BFO (0.139 cm3/g). This indicates that the BFO/CC has higher reactive sites and their large surface area helps to enhance the photocatalytic activity [60,61].

The surface chemical composition and chemical state of BFO/CC were studied by XPS. The XPS spectrum in Fig. 5 a shows elemental peaks of Bi, Fe, O, C, and N. Fig. 5b shows that the binding energies corresponding to the two strong peaks of Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 are 164.04 and 158.75 eV, respectively. Fig. 5c reveals the binding energies of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 at 710.74 and 724.34 eV, respectively. Fig. 5d shows the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1 s, with two different energy peaks appearing at 529.43 and 530.43 eV respectively, which are attributed to lattice oxygen atoms and surface adsorbed oxygen atoms, agreed with ref. [62]. In Fig. 5e, the peak at 287.93 eV is caused by the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in g-C3N4, and the peak at 284.85 eV belongs to the CC bond in C-dots, same as ref. [35]. The N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5f) can be divided into three peaks located at 398.67, 398.98 and 399.76 eV It is worth noting that the characteristic peak of C = NC bonding in g-C3N4 is split into two peaks (398.67 and 398.98 eV), indicating the interaction between g-C3N4 and C-dots [63]. The peak at 399.76 eV is associated with the N atoms, as in ref. [64].

To study the optical absorption properties, UV–vis spectra of the samples were performed. As shown in Fig. 6 a, compared to BFO, the light absorption range of BFO/CC is wider and the absorption intensity is enhanced, indicating that the ability to capture light has been enhanced, which is beneficial to the photocatalytic activity. In general, the light absorption properties of semiconductors are evaluated based on the band gap energy (Eg). The Eg of each sample in Fig. 6b has been calculated by the following formula: (αhν) n = k(hν-Eg), where α is the absorption coefficient of the material, k is the effective mass-related parameters with valence band and conduction band, n is 2 means indirect transition, hν is the absorption energy, and Eg is the band gap energy [65]. Thus, the band gaps of g-C3N4 and Bi2Fe4O9 are estimated to be 2.70 eV and 2.20 eV, respectively, which are consistent with literature reports [34]. According to the band gaps, the corresponding valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) potentials have been calculated by the following relations [66]:

In the above equations, X is the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor [3,65,67], Ee is the energy of free electrons at the hydrogen atom scale (4.5 eV), and Eg is the band gap energy of the semiconductor.

Table 1 lists the calculated energy levels of g-C3N4, Bi2Fe4O9. Based on the above formulas, it can be calculated that the CB and VB of g-C3N4 are −1.13 eV and 1.57 eV, and the CB and VB of Bi2Fe4O9 are 0.40 eV and 2.60 eV, respectively.

Electrochemical measurements are used to determine whether unpaired electrons increase photocurrent density and electron transfer efficiency. Fig. 7 a shows the I-t curves of BFO and BFO/CC under several switching cycles of simulated intermittent light exposure. Both BFO and BFO/CC show obvious photocurrent when the light is ON and the photocurrent response still exists even after several ON/OFF light cycles. The higher photocurrent of BFO/CC shows that BFO/CC exhibits a stronger photocurrent response than BFO, which indicates that BFO/CC is more efficient in electron-hole separation and photocatalytic reaction, more conducive to the transmission of photogenerated carriers and the inhibition of electron/hole recombination [68,69]. In addition, the results of EIS Nyquist plots (Fig. 7b) show that the arc radius of BFO/CC is smaller than that of BFO, indicating a lower charge-transfer resistance and faster photogenerated charge migration through the interface [70].

SPV is also an effective method to reveal the electron-hole separation efficiency of photocatalysts. The results show that BFO/CC exhibited a higher signal than BFO in the range of 300–600 nm (Fig. 7c). This indicates that BFO/CC has a strong photovoltage-induced response, and better charge separation after recombination, thus showing higher photogenerated carrier separation efficiency [71,72].

PL is also used to study the separation efficiency of photo-generated carriers. Lower photoluminescence intensity indicates better separation efficiency of photoexcited electron-hole pair and higher photocatalytic behavior [73]. In Fig. 7d, the PL emission intensity of the ternary composite BFO/CC heterostructure is significantly lowered compared to the pure BFO. The PL signal of BFO/CC shows that the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs are effectively inhibited, which indicates that the formation of the BFO/CC heterostructure is beneficial to promote the separation and transfer of photocarriers and improving the photocatalytic activity [74,75]. In addition, compared to the pristine BFO, the emission peak of BFO/CC shows a red-shift from 391 nm to 434 nm owing to the band gap being different when forming a heterostructure, as illustrated in Fig. 6 [76].

In our previous work, the g-C3N4 has been optimized with C-dots modification and achieved the largest surface area when the ratio of C-dots/g-C3N4 is 3:10 [52]. Hence, herein, the C-dots/g-C3N4 ratio has been fixed at 3:10 first and then the ratio of BFO to C-dots/g-C3N4 has been explored. The mass ratio between BFO, C-dots and g-C3N4 has been explored to maximize the piezoelectric activity of BFO/CC under stirring (625 rpm). It is worth noting that the stirring speed in the first 30 min is higher than that in the degradation process. All the measurements have been kept in darkness to avoid photocatalysis. Fig. 8 shows the piezoelectric catalytic activity with different composition ratios. Under the same experimental conditions, over 100 min, the degradation rate of RhB with the BFO/CC of 38:1:3.3 mass ratio reached 83.8%, while that of RhB with BFO (red line) is just over 50%. Therefore, the BFO/CC with a mass ratio of BFO: C-dots: g-C3N4=38: 1: 3.3 (1.5 g: 0.039 g: 0.13 g) has been employed for further study.

To evaluate the synergistic effect of photocatalytic and piezoelectric effect of BFO/CC, we compared the degradation of RhB under weak UV, visible light, mechanical stirring, and light irradiation with stirring. The degradation efficiency of both pristine BFO and BFO/CC is quite low under weak UV and visible light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 9 a, after weak UV irradiation for 180 min, the degradation rate of RhB with pristine BFO is lower than 4%, and the degradation rate with BFO/CC composite (about 10%) is just slightly higher. Similarly, in Fig. 9b, the degradation of RhB under visible light with pristine BFO (about 2.6%) is also lower than that of BFO/CC (about 6%). Therefore, the fabrication of BFO/CC can slightly promote photocatalytic activity.

Owing to the piezoelectric properties of BFO, both pristine BFO and BFO/CC show much higher degradation efficiency under constant stirring with an RhB degradation rate of 65% and 67% over 180 min, respectively (Fig. 9a, b). Surprisingly, the synergistic photo-piezoelectric performance with both BFO and BFO/CC is not simply the sum up of piezo and photocatalytic activity but has been significantly promoted. Over 180 min, the RhB degradation rate reached over 93.19% and 94.16% under weak UV and visible light irradiation with piezoelectric effect, respectively.

Although both BFO and BFO/CC show excellent photo-piezoelectric activity, the RhB degradation efficiencies are different. To provide a more direct comparison, the above degradation rates are quantitatively presented by comparing their reaction rate constant k in Fig. 9c, d, which is defined as:
(3)
k = l n

C 0
/ C
)
/ t

Where, C0 is the initial concentration of RhB, and C is the concentration of RhB at time t. The photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis of BFO and the visible light photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis of BFO/CC are polynomial third-order fits, and the rest are linear fits. The photocatalysis, piezoelectric catalysis and photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis are fitted and compared in Fig. 9c, d. The slopes of BFO/CC under the photo-, piezo- and photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis are all higher than those of BFO. However, the photo-piezoelectric synergistic catalysis rate of BFO flats out after 120 min, while BFO/CC still maintains a high catalytic rate, which indicates a better enduring catalytic activity of BFO/CC over BFO.

The higher catalytic activity of BFO/CC over BFO is consistent with the degradation curves. This is because the formation of heterojunction between the BFO, C-dots, and g-C3N4 extended the recombination path of the photo-generated electron-hole pair and the smaller band gap allows the electrons and holes generated by light with higher wavelength and lower power. And also, the built-in electric field of BFO under stirring inhibited the recombination of electron-hole pairs. As a result, under the synergistic photo-piezoelectric conditions, BFO/CC shows 15.43- and 1.41- times higher degradation rates than that under weak light irradiation and stirring only. To evaluate the BFO/CC application potentials for the degradation of other pollution, a typical antibiotic, TC, has been investigated by a UV–vis spectrophotometer at a wavelength of 352 nm. According to ref. [77–80], the trend of degradation rate measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer and the TOC change by a TOC monitor is mostly the same. As illustrated in Fig. 10 , over 180 min, the TC degradation rate reaches over 47.92% under visible light irradiation with the piezoelectric effect. This suggests that BFO/CC is also effective for other degradation systems, and the catalytic potential of BFO/CC for multiple pollutions.

In addition to the activity of the catalyst, stability and recyclability are the other two important parameters for catalysis. The piezoelectric catalysis experiment was repeated 5 times with 10 mg catalyst and 50 ml organic pollutant solution to test its stability. In Fig. 11 , both BFO and BFO/CC show no significant deactivation over five cycles, indicating good stability, endurance, and recyclability. The details of the cyclic stability test are provided in the ESI.

The above experimental results indicate that both BFO and BFO/CC have excellent synergistic photo-piezoelectric even under weak light irradiation, and by loading BFO and C-dots onto the two-dimensional lamellar structure g-C3N4, the synergistic catalytic activity can be further improved. To further clarify the catalytic mechanism of BFO and BFO/CC, and determine whether the catalytic mechanism changed before and after the formation of BFO/CC heterostructure, active species capture experiments have been investigated. Details of the trapping experiment are provided in the ESI. Four scarves, tert‑butanol TBA (capture of •OH), EDTA-2Na (capture of h +), p-benzoquinone p-BQ (capture of •O2 −), and AgNO3 (capture of e −), have been used to identify the main active substances in the catalytic reaction process [81,82]. As for the BFO, it can be seen from Fig. 12 a to c that both BQ and TBA captured the active species under UV irradiation, EDTA-2Na captured the active species under stirring, and TBA captured the active species under synergistic catalytic conditions. These results indicate that •O2 − and •OH are the main free radicals under UV irradiation, h + is the main free radicals under piezoelectric condition, while with the synergistic photo-piezoelectric condition, the •OH and h + are the free radicals.

However, the BFO/CC shows a different photo-piezoelectric catalytic mechanism. As in Fig. 12d to e, similar to that of pristine BFO, BQ and EDTA-2Na have captured •O2 − and h + under UV irradiation and piezoelectric condition, respectively. While under the synergistic condition (Fig. 12f), with BFO/CC, the BQ (capture of •O2 −) and EDTA-2Na (capture of h +), instead of TBA (capture of •OH) with pristine BFO, show the best free radical capture performance. This result indicates a different catalytic mechanism between BFO and BFO/CC under the synergistic photo-piezoelectric mechanism. It is noticeable that adding AgNO3 (capture e −) leads to a significantly higher photocatalytic efficiency [83–85]. This is because the AgNO3 captured e − and minimized the e − and h + recombination, which leads to a better h + oriented catalytic. AgNO3 (capture e −) is thermally reduced to Ag nanoparticles under the irradiation of UV irradiation, which produces the EPR effect and enhanced the catalytic activity [86]. In addition, O3 is produced owing to AgNO3, which promotes the decomposition of dyes by converting the polymer compounds of dyes into low molecular organic matter, and its decomposition is even more effective under UV irradiation [87–90]. Therefore, adding AgNO3 to the system can enhance photocatalytic activity. However, considering the impact on environmental problems, we proposed a metal-free catalyst without employing AgNO3 despite its excellent catalytic activity.

The active free radicals of BFO/CC under illumination and piezoelectric conditions are •O2 − and h +, respectively, while the active radicals under synergistic conditions are •O2 − and h +. Based on the experiments, a possible catalytic mechanism of S-scheme heterojunction BFO/CC has been proposed in Fig. 13 . Under light irradiation, electrons and holes are generated (Eq. (4)). Excited electrons on CB of BFO will spontaneously transfer to VB of g-C3N4 under an electric field with band bending and combine with the holes. C-dots act as a bridge, acting as an efficient electronic medium between BFO and g-C3N4. Meanwhile, holes on the VB of BFO and electrons on the CB of g-C3N4 will accumulate. Thus, the carrier transport path of S-type heterojunction can effectively inhibit charge recombination, and preserve a strong redox ability [91,92]. In detail, under light irradiation, the accumulated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 participate in the molecular oxygen reaction to produce •O2 − active substance (Eq. (5)). Under the piezoelectric condition, the holes accumulated in VB of BFO will react with water to form •OH (Eq. (6)). Meanwhile, the reactive substance produced •O2 − and the holes accumulated in VB of BFO have redox reactions with RhB and TC (Eq. (7)).

In conclusion, the BFO and BFO/CC heterostructures have been synthesized via the hydrothermal method and their photo-piezoelectric catalytic activity has been systemically investigated. High-efficiency degradation towards organic dye and antibiotics in wastewater has been achieved with the synergistic photo-piezoelectric effect, where piezoelectric polarization of BFO under stirring improved the photo-generated electron-hole separation, and the loading of 0-D BFO and C-dots on the 2-D g-C3N4 with opened layer distance ensured a large surface area and extended the electron transfer path, which all contributed to the better synergistic catalytic activity even with very low light irradiation power (only 12.2 mW/cm2, 420 nm). The difference in catalytic mechanism between BFO and BFO/CC has been compared, and the formation of BFO/CC heterostructure shows better degradation endurance with higher catalytic efficiency. The excellent synergistic photo-piezoelectric performance under weak visible light and stirring treatment may fully use sunlight and wave energy and significantly enhance wastewater degradation under various natural water conditions, such as in the ocean and rivers. The design of ternary composite material in this study will also provide a solution for the optimization of photo-piezoelectric materials.
